text:
<think>I should start by briefly introducing that the word "crap" has a complex and interesting etymology.</think>
The word "crap" has a somewhat complicated origin, involving several languages and historical developments.  
<think>Now I should explain that the word's modern sense is not directly tied to its most famous association, the name Thomas Crapper.</think>
Contrary to popular belief, the word is not derived from Thomas Crapper, the 19th-century English plumber associated with toilet fixtures.  
<think>I should clarify where the word actually comes from, mentioning its roots in earlier languages.</think>
Instead, "crap" likely comes from the Middle English word "crappe," which referred to chaff or residue.  
<think>Providing the linguistic lineage will help clarify the development of the word.</think>
This Middle English term itself was borrowed from Old French "crappe," meaning siftings or waste, and ultimately from Medieval Latin "crappa."  
<think>It would be helpful to connect how the meaning shifted towards its modern usage.</think>
Over time, the meaning shifted from referring to waste or discarded matter in general to a vulgar term for feces and, more broadly, something of poor quality.  
<think>Summarizing the main point can help wrap up the explanation.</think>
In summary, the word "crap" evolved from words meaning "chaff" or "waste" and was not originally a vulgar term.
